does: This paper proposes Calibrated Self-Rewarding (CSR), a method that enables Vision-Language Models to self-improve by iteratively generating candidate responses, evaluating rewards internally, and using those self-generated preferences for training refinement. Rather than relying on external models or human annotations to create preference data, the model learns to reward its own outputs based on vision-language alignment, addressing the hallucination problem where generated text contradicts input images. The approach calibrates the reward signal to reflect the target model's own preferences, making the preference data more aligned with what the model actually learns to optimize for.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in preference-based optimization for language models, extending methods like DPO (Direct Preference Optimization) and self-reward mechanisms to the multimodal setting. It responds to the long-standing problem of modality imbalance in vision-language models, where text generation often dominates over visual grounding despite integration of high-quality vision encoders and language models. The paper addresses limitations of prior approaches that curate preferences using separate reward models or human annotations, which may not reflect the target model's actual learning dynamics. It connects to broader research on instruction tuning, multimodal alignment, and the challenge of distinguishing genuine understanding from linguistic plausibility in generated outputs.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more efficient self-improving multimodal systems that reduce dependence on external annotation pipelines or auxiliary models. The calibrated self-reward mechanism could extend beyond vision-language alignment to other modality combinations or cross-modal understanding tasks. The method suggests how models might achieve better alignment by iteratively refining their own reward signals, potentially inspiring similar self-improvement loops in other domains. Future work could explore combining CSR with other preference optimization techniques, scaling to larger model families, or applying the framework to emerging multimodal tasks beyond image captioning and visual question answering.
